serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Ngoh Hiang consists of 3 to 4 pieces totalling approximately 150 g, as commonly served at hawker centres and zi char stalls in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Ngo Hiang / Five-spice pork roll — nutrient composition estimated from HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central component data for minced pork, prawn, water chestnut, beancurd skin, and deep-frying oil absorption
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single exact entry for Ngoh Hiang was found in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated by component analysis: minced pork (~50%), prawn (~15%), water chestnut (~10%), beancurd skin (~10%), oil absorption from deep-frying (~10%), and seasoning (~5%). Energy and macros are cross-referenced against published Singapore hawker food composition studies and the IMR Malaysia MyFCD entry for similar five-spice meat rolls. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed meat and beancurd skin rolls estimated from analogous protein-fat dominant fried food; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Ngoh Hiang exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous high-protein, moderate-fat fried meat rolls with beancurd skin wrapper. The low carbohydrate content (~10.5 g/100g), high protein, and fat content suppress glycemic response. Analogous reference: fried dim sum dumplings and meat-filled beancurd rolls, GI range 30–40 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. GL per 150 g serving = (35 × 10.5 × 1.5) / 100 = 5.5.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~520 mg/100g; ~780 mg per 150 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Ngoh Hiang intake due to its high sodium content from seasoning and soy-based marinade; a single serving can contribute approximately 34% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate content with low glycemic index (~35), but high fat and caloric density
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ngoh Hiang has a low glycemic index (~35) and moderate carbohydrate load, making acute glucose excursion risk relatively low; however, its high fat content and caloric density (~245 kcal/100g) require portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes managing weight and lipid profiles.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (~5.2 g/100g), high sodium, and deep-fried preparation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of saturated fat from pork, oil absorption from deep-frying, and high sodium content makes Ngoh Hiang a food of concern for individuals with cardiovascular disease; consumption should be infrequent and portion-limited in line with Singapore HPB heart-healthy dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ngoh Hiang (五香, also romanised as Ngo Hiang or Lor Bak) is a traditional Hokkien-Chinese fried meat roll widely popular in Singapore, Malaysia, and among overseas Hokkien communities. The dish consists of a seasoned filling of minced pork, prawns, and water chestnuts flavoured with five-spice powder (wu xiang fen), wrapped tightly in dried beancurd skin (tofu skin) and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The exterior is characteristically crunchy while the interior remains moist and savoury, with a distinctive aromatic warmth from the five-spice blend of star anise, cloves, cinnamon, Sichuan pepper, and fennel seeds. Ngoh Hiang is commonly served at Singapore hawker centres, zi char stalls, and as a festive dish during Chinese New Year and other celebrations. It is typically accompanied by a sweet chilli dipping sauce or a dark soy-based dipping sauce. Nutritionally, Ngoh Hiang is a calorie-dense, protein-moderate, high-fat food (~245 kcal/100g, 14.5 g protein, 16 g fat) with significant sodium content (~520 mg/100g) due to seasoning. Its glycemic index is low (~35) owing to the protein-fat dominant composition and minimal refined carbohydrate content. Clinically, it is of concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to sodium and saturated fat levels, and should be consumed in moderation by individuals managing these conditions.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Ngoh Hiang?
What is Ngo Hiang made of?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Ngoh Hiang was found in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from component analysis of primary ingredients (minced pork, prawn, water chestnut, beancurd skin, deep-frying oil absorption) cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD and published Singapore hawker food studies. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database reference foods. Confidence is medium.
notes: Nutrient values are component-estimated; no single approved-source entry for Ngoh Hiang was identified. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for this estimation. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for this estimation. Trans fat is reported as 0.1 g/100g reflecting minor naturally occurring trans fats from pork fat; this is not a confirmed zero. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on protein-fat dominant fried meat rolls from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. The food_name has been normalised from the input menu label 'B7 Ngoh Hiang 五香' to the canonical English name 'Ngoh Hiang'; the menu prefix 'B7' is a restaurant ordering code and is not part of the canonical food identity. Cuisine has been reclassified from 'Malaysian' to 'Singaporean Chinese' as Ngoh Hiang is a Hokkien-Chinese dish with deep roots in Singapore hawker culture, though it is also found in Malaysia. The input cuisine_tags included 'Bak Kut Teh' and 'Soup' which are not applicable to Ngoh Hiang and have been disregarded.
